title: Grappling Foundations & Live Training
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance

required.title: [Instruction] Regular BJJ and/or wrestling classes with a qualified instructor
[For Mentor] The Guard
required.author: Joe Moreira and *Drill to Win* by Andre Galvao

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: John Danaher instructionals (short free clips) or Lachlan Giles fundamentals
[Video] YouTube: Wrestling highlights from NCAA or international competition

competence_markers.description: The student can demonstrate at least two takedowns (e.g., double-leg and single-leg) against moderate resistance
The student can explain the positional hierarchy and identify positions during live rolling
The student demonstrates a functional guard game — maintains guard, attempts sweeps, and threatens submissions from bottom
The student can execute at least one guard pass with reasonable success against a resisting partner
The student can demonstrate and explain at least four submissions (e.g., rear naked choke, armbar, triangle, kimura)
The student rolls regularly with composure — they do not panic, rage-quit, or refuse to roll with challenging partners
The student adjusts intensity appropriately for different training partners
The student helps newer students on the mat without being asked

cross_connections.description: The mat is IC1's primary testing ground. How you handle loss, how you treat partners, whether you cut corners — all visible, all revealing.
Sparring is controlled adversity. Being submitted is controlled failure. The entire training methodology is IC3 applied physically.
Grip strength, core strength, and conditioning directly improve grappling performance. The two packets reinforce each other.
Martial capability is the combat layer of tactical fitness. The skills in this packet are what you apply when 2.1.5's situational awareness identifies a threat that cannot be avoided.
Helping newer students, adjusting intensity for partners, and coaching basics are leadership skills practiced in a physical context.

content.why_this_matters:
You are about to begin the part of martial arts training that changes everything: live rolling.

Until now, you have been learning positions, practicing techniques in drills, and getting comfortable with physical contact. That was the foundation, and it was necessary. But drilling is practice against a cooperating partner. Live rolling is practice against someone who is genuinely trying to stop you. This difference is enormous.

When you roll live for the first time, you will discover something uncomfortable: techniques that work perfectly in drilling fall apart when your partner resists. The armbar you could hit every time on a cooperative partner suddenly does not work when your partner is fighting the grip, posturing up, and stacking you. The takedown that felt crisp in practice stalls when your partner sprawls and crossfaces you. This is not a failure of your technique. It is the discovery that technique must be forged under pressure before it is reliable.

This is the forge. Every martial artist who has ever been genuinely competent went through this phase — the phase where they got submitted constantly, where stronger or more experienced partners controlled them at will, where they realized how little they actually knew. The students who survive this phase and keep showing up become capable. The students who quit because losing hurts their ego stay unskilled forever.

You are also going to develop a detailed understanding of grappling — Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu and wrestling — at a level of technical depth that most adults never reach. You will learn takedowns, guard work, passing, submissions, and the positional hierarchy that governs the entire grappling game. This is the most practical self-defense training available, because most real-world physical confrontations end up in a grapple, and the person who knows how to control position on the ground controls the outcome.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **A functional takedown game** — single-leg, double-leg, body-lock, and trip series for bringing an opponent to the ground
2. **Guard play** — closed guard, butterfly guard, and basic open guard; sweeps and submissions from bottom position
3. **Passing** — pressure passing and speed passing concepts; getting past an opponent's legs to a dominant position
4. **The submission game** — rear naked choke, guillotine, armbar, triangle, kimura, americana as mechanical puzzles
5. **Positional hierarchy understanding** — the map of positions and transitions that governs all grappling
6. **Live rolling competence** — the ability to grapple at full speed against a resisting partner with composure
7. **Ego management** — handling loss, helping newer students, and training with curiosity rather than desperation
8. **Wrestling intensity** — the aggressive, pace-driven style of wrestling that develops different qualities than BJJ
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Positional Hierarchy — The Map of Grappling

Every grappling exchange exists somewhere on a map of positions. Understanding this map is the single most important conceptual skill in BJJ and wrestling, because it tells you where you are, where you want to go, and what your opponent is trying to do.

**The hierarchy, from most dominant to least dominant:**

1. **Back mount (with hooks and seatbelt grip)** — You are behind your opponent with your legs hooked around their waist and your arms controlling their upper body. This is the most dominant position in grappling. Your opponent cannot see you, cannot strike you effectively, and you have access to the most powerful submission in the art — the rear naked choke. When you have someone's back, you are winning. When someone has your back, you are in serious trouble.

2. **Mount** — You are sitting on your opponent's torso with your knees on the mat. Gravity works for you. You can strike (in a real situation) or attack submissions. Your opponent's arms are occupied with defense. High mount (chest/shoulders) is more dominant than low mount (waist).

3. **Side control** — You are chest-to-chest with your opponent, perpendicular to their body, with your weight on them. A strong position for control and submission attacks. Slightly less dominant than mount because your opponent has more movement options.

4. **Half guard (top)** — You have passed one of your opponent's legs but they have trapped your other leg between theirs. You are in a dominant position, but your opponent has a path to recovery. Your job from top half guard is to free your trapped leg and advance to side control or mount.

5. **Guard (various forms)** — Your opponent is between your legs. In BJJ, this is not a defeated position — it is an offensive position with sweeps, submissions, and the potential to reverse the situation. But you are still on bottom, and in a street fight, being on your back is a significant disadvantage. Guard is where you fight from if you end up there, not where you want to stay.

6. **Turtle** — You are on all fours with your opponent on top of or beside you. A defensive position that protects your neck and buys time, but you are vulnerable to back takes and certain submissions. Turtle is a transitional position — you use it briefly while working to a better position.

7. **Bottom side control, bottom mount, opponent has your back** — You are in a bad position. Your priority is survival (protect your neck, manage posture) and escape (create space, shrimp to guard recovery, turn into your opponent).

**How to use this map:** At any point during a roll, ask yourself: where am I on the hierarchy? If you are higher, your job is to maintain or advance. If you are lower, your job is to escape to a better position. Every technique you learn — every sweep, pass, submission, and escape — moves you on this map.

### Part 2: Takedowns — Bringing the Fight to the Ground

In a real confrontation, the fight starts standing. The person who controls where the fight happens — standing or on the ground — has a massive advantage. Takedowns are how you put the fight on the ground on your terms.

**The double-leg takedown:** The highest-percentage takedown in wrestling. From your stance, you change levels (drop your hips), shoot forward, wrapping both arms around your opponent's legs above the knees. Drive your shoulder into their hip, your head against their side, and run your feet through them. They fall because their base has been completely eliminated — you have both their legs, and they have nothing to stand on.

**Key details:** Your head goes to the outside (not between their legs — that exposes you to a guillotine choke). Your hands lock behind their knees. Your feet keep moving forward — do not stop your feet. A double-leg with dead feet stalls. A double-leg with driving feet finishes.

**The single-leg takedown:** Grab one of your opponent's legs — typically the lead leg — and control it. Variations for finishing: run the pipe (lift the leg and circle toward their back), the trip (hook their standing leg with yours), or the dump (drive into them while off-balancing them over the trapped leg).

The single-leg is lower commitment than the double-leg — you expose less of yourself — but it requires more wrestling skill to finish because your opponent still has one leg to base on.

**The body-lock takedown:** From the clinch (standing chest-to-chest), wrap your arms around your opponent's torso, lock your hands, and take them down by tripping, lifting, or twisting. The body lock is simple and effective, especially for students who are naturally strong.

**Trip series:** Inside trip, outside trip — using your legs to sweep your opponent's legs while you drive into them from the clinch. Trips require less athleticism than shots and are effective against larger opponents.

**Wrestling drills:** Takedowns require thousands of repetitions. Drill them from stance — shot drills, level-change drills, penetration steps. Then drill them against light resistance. Then against increasing resistance. Then in live takedown sparring. This is a months-to-years process. There are no shortcuts.

### Part 3: Guard — Fighting From Your Back

Guard is the most revolutionary concept in BJJ. Every other martial art views being on your back as a catastrophe. BJJ treats it as a legitimate fighting position — and from guard, you can sweep your opponent (reverse the position so you are on top), submit them (force a tap), or stand up and return to your feet.

**Closed guard:** Your legs are wrapped around your opponent's waist, ankles crossed behind their back. This is the most fundamental guard. You control their posture by pulling their head down with your hands (breaking their posture), which prevents them from striking or passing. From closed guard, you attack with armbars, triangles, sweeps, and kimuras.

**Butterfly guard:** You are seated in front of your opponent with your feet hooked inside their thighs (your shins against their inner legs). This is a dynamic guard built around sweeps — using your hooks to elevate and off-balance your opponent. The butterfly sweep (hook under their thigh, off-balance them to one side, come up on top) is one of the highest-percentage techniques in all of BJJ.

**Open guard basics:** Any guard position where your legs are not locked around your opponent. You use your feet, knees, and shins to create frames and hooks that prevent your opponent from passing. Open guard is more complex than closed guard — there are dozens of open guard systems — but the principle is always the same: use your legs to control distance and prevent your opponent from getting past your legs to side control.

**The key concept:** Guard is not a rest position. If you sit in guard and wait, a good opponent will pass. Guard is an active position — you are constantly threatening sweeps and submissions, which forces your opponent to react defensively, which creates the openings you need. A passive guard player gets passed. An active guard player controls the match.

### Part 4: Passing — Getting Past the Guard

If guard is a door, passing is opening it. Your opponent is using their legs to keep you from advancing to side control or mount. Your job is to get past those legs — through them, around them, or over them.

**Pressure passing:** Use your body weight, posture, and forward drive to collapse your opponent's guard frames. Pin their legs, flatten their hips, and grind your way to side control. Pressure passing is physically demanding and mentally oppressive for the bottom player. It works well for larger, stronger grapplers but is available to everyone.

**Speed passing:** Use footwork, angles, and timing to get around your opponent's guard before they can react. Torreando pass (grab both pant legs or ankles, push the legs to one side, step around to the other side), leg drag (pull one leg across your body and step past it). Speed passing rewards timing and agility.

**The principle:** Every guard has a weakness. Every pass exploits a specific weakness. As you advance, you will learn to read which guard your opponent is playing and select the appropriate passing strategy. For now, develop competence in at least one pressure pass and one speed pass, and drill both relentlessly.

### Part 5: Submissions — The Checkmate

Submissions are how grappling matches end. A submission is a hold — a choke or a joint lock — that your opponent cannot escape, forcing them to tap or face injury (joint lock) or unconsciousness (choke). Submissions are checkmates: the culmination of positional control, timing, and technique.

**Rear naked choke:** The highest-percentage submission in all of grappling and MMA. From back mount, you slide one arm under your opponent's chin, grip your own bicep on the other arm, and place the hand of the second arm behind their head. Squeeze by expanding your chest and pulling your elbows together. This compresses the carotid arteries on both sides of the neck, producing unconsciousness in seconds if not released. It does not crush the windpipe (when done correctly) — it restricts blood flow to the brain.

**Armbar:** From mount or guard, you isolate one of your opponent's arms, trap it between your thighs, and extend it against the natural bend of the elbow. The fulcrum is your hips. If the opponent does not tap, the elbow hyperextends. This is a joint lock — it attacks the structure of the arm.

**Triangle choke:** From guard, you trap your opponent's head and one arm between your legs, forming a triangle shape. Your legs compress the carotid arteries on one side while your opponent's own trapped shoulder compresses the other side. A blood choke using your legs.

**Guillotine choke:** From a front headlock (your arm wrapped around your opponent's neck from the front, their head under your armpit), you squeeze by pulling your forearm into the front of their throat while controlling their posture. Can be applied standing or from guard.

**Kimura and americana:** Shoulder locks that attack the rotator cuff. The kimura (from guard or side control) involves gripping your opponent's wrist and rotating their arm behind their back. The americana (from mount or side control) is the reverse rotation. Both force a tap or risk shoulder injury.

**The principle of submissions:** You rarely catch a submission from a bad position. Submissions come from positional dominance. The sequence is: achieve a dominant position → control that position → attack submissions from that position. A student who jumps for submissions without establishing position will miss most of them and get swept or passed in the process.

### Part 6: Live Rolling — The Crucible

Live rolling is grappling at full speed against a partner who is genuinely trying to submit you while you try to submit them. It is where everything you have drilled is tested, and where you discover the difference between knowing a technique and being able to apply it.

**What live rolling teaches that drilling cannot:**

- **Timing.** The armbar works not because you know the steps but because you execute it at the exact moment your opponent's posture breaks.
- **Pressure management.** What it actually feels like to have someone's weight on your chest. How to breathe and think under physical pressure.
- **Problem-solving in real time.** Your opponent is not waiting for you to figure out your next move. You must recognize positions, recall techniques, and execute under time pressure and physical stress simultaneously.
- **Ego calibration.** You will be submitted by people who are smaller, younger, lighter, and less athletic than you — because they are more skilled. This is the most powerful humility training in the framework.

**How to roll well:**

- **Roll to learn, not to win.** Every roll has a lesson. If you are only rolling to submit your opponent, you miss the lessons. If you get caught, ask your partner what they did. Try new techniques even when they might fail. Use rolling as a laboratory, not a war.
- **Match your intensity to your partner.** If you are rolling with someone smaller or newer, roll lighter. If you are rolling with someone bigger or more experienced, match their intensity. The ability to adjust your intensity is a sign of maturity.
- **Protect your training partners.** If you catch a submission, apply it slowly enough for your partner to tap. If your partner is in a bad position, do not crank a joint lock or choke as hard as you can. Your partner is not your enemy — they are the person who makes your training possible. Injuring them is stupid, not tough.
- **Roll with everyone.** Not just people you can beat. Not just people your size. Roll with the purple belt who submits you twelve times in five minutes. Roll with the new kid who does not know anything yet (and help them). Roll with the person whose game gives you the most trouble. Comfort is the enemy of growth.

### Part 7: Wrestling — The Other Half of the Grappling Game

BJJ and wrestling develop different but complementary skills. BJJ specializes in ground control and submissions. Wrestling specializes in takedowns, top pressure, and the relentless pace that breaks opponents.

**What wrestling adds to your game:**

- **Aggression and pace.** Wrestling is trained at a higher intensity than most BJJ classes. The pace is faster. The effort is more sustained. Wrestling builds the gas tank and mental toughness that many BJJ practitioners lack.
- **Takedown superiority.** Wrestlers have the best takedown skills of any grappling art. Period. A wrestler who trains BJJ submissions is an extremely complete grappler.
- **Top pressure.** Wrestlers learn to make their weight felt. A 130-pound wrestler on top of you feels like 200 pounds because they know how to distribute their weight for maximum pressure.
- **Scrambles.** The chaotic, fast transitions that happen when neither person has established a position. Wrestlers excel in scrambles because their sport rewards speed and improvisation in transition.

**Training wrestling:** Pummeling drills (fighting for underhooks in the clinch), takedown sparring (live wrestling from standing only), and stance-and-motion work. If a wrestling club is available, train there alongside BJJ. If not, dedicate time in every grappling session to takedown work.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Takedown Competence
**Developing well:** The student shoots takedowns with proper level changes, penetration steps, and finishing mechanics. They can take down a cooperating partner consistently and have moderate success against resisting partners. They initiate takedowns in live rolling rather than pulling guard.

**Developing slowly:** The student avoids standing exchanges. They pull guard immediately or wait to be taken down. Their takedown attempts lack level changes or finishing mechanics — they grab a leg and stall.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting sufficient takedown drilling? Is the academy emphasizing takedowns, or is it a guard-pull culture? If the student is only doing BJJ without wrestling-specific training, consider adding wrestling practice.

### 2. Positional Understanding
**Developing well:** During live rolling, the student recognizes positions and acts with purpose. They attempt to advance from dominant positions, escape from inferior positions, and use positional control to set up submissions. They can watch a match and narrate the positional flow.

**Developing slowly:** The student grapples randomly — grabbing and pulling without a positional strategy. They do not seem to know whether they are in a good or bad position. They hold positions without advancing or submit from inferior positions by luck rather than design.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you quizzing positional awareness after rolling sessions? Ask: "Where were you when he submitted you? What position should you have been trying to get to? What happened in the transition?" This builds the analytical habit.

### 3. Composure Under Pressure
**Developing well:** The student rolls with controlled breathing, maintains a technical approach even when losing, and recovers emotionally after being submitted. They can be pinned under heavy top pressure without panicking. They manage frustration without outbursts.

**Developing slowly:** The student panics when pinned, holds their breath during rolling, becomes visibly angry after being submitted, or stops training when they are losing. They use excessive strength as a substitute for technique.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student been exposed to positional sparring from bad positions? Putting them in bottom side control and asking them to escape builds comfort with pressure over time. Are they breathing? Remind them to exhale during every exchange. Are they sleeping enough? Emotional dysregulation during training often traces to insufficient sleep or high stress outside the mat.

### 4. Treatment of Training Partners
**Developing well:** The student rolls lighter with newer or smaller partners. They help others without being asked. They apply submissions slowly enough for a tap. They thank training partners after rounds. They are sought out as a rolling partner because they are safe and helpful.

**Developing slowly:** The student hurts or intimidates smaller partners. They celebrate submissions aggressively. They avoid helping newer students. They are avoided as a rolling partner because they are rough or unsafe.

**Diagnostic questions:** This is an IC1 issue, not a technical one. Address it directly: "Your training partners are not your opponents. They are the people who make your training possible. If you injure them or drive them away, you have fewer people to train with, and everyone loses." If the behavior persists, restrict live rolling privileges until the attitude shifts.
